question: Why do you want to study Biology specifically?
lookingFor: A genuine intellectual motivation grounded in curiosity about living systems, not just a vague desire to help people or work in healthcare.

bullets: Identify a specific aspect of biology — a concept, a moment, a question — that hooked you
Distinguish the pull of biology from adjacent fields like medicine or chemistry
Show the curiosity is ongoing, not just a school memory
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The moment that hooked me was dissecting a heart in year eleven and realising I couldn't stop thinking about how it knew to beat in the right rhythm without being told to. That question — how do cells coordinate without a conductor — led me into reading about signalling pathways and eventually into my EPQ on circadian rhythms. Medicine felt too clinically focused for where my curiosity sits, and chemistry felt too removed from the organism level. Biology is the right lens for me because it asks questions at every scale, from a single protein to a whole ecosystem, and I want to spend the next few years learning to move between those scales.

commonMistakes: Saying you love science generally without specifying biology
Framing motivation entirely around a future career rather than genuine intellectual interest
A motivation that could equally describe medicine or biochemistry
followUps: Which area of biology are you most curious about right now?

question: What drew you to study Biology at this university in particular?
lookingFor: Evidence the applicant has researched beyond rankings — specific modules, research groups, fieldwork, or facilities they can name and connect to their own interests.

bullets: Name something specific: a module, a research strength, a lab facility, or a staff member's work
Connect it clearly to your own intellectual interest or longer-term goal
Show you understand what this course offers that a similar course elsewhere does not
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I was drawn in by the field ecology module in the second year, which is structured around a research station in the Scottish Highlands — that kind of embedded fieldwork is rare at undergraduate level and it matches exactly how I learn best, by being in the environment I'm trying to understand. I also read a paper from the department's plant stress physiology group that I found genuinely surprising; the idea that drought-stressed plants alter their root microbiome composition to recruit specific bacteria is the kind of cross-scale interaction I want to study. I'd hope to get involved with that group during my third year project.

commonMistakes: Generic praise about 'excellent facilities' or 'world-leading research' with no substance
Choosing a feature that every comparable university has
Confusing departments or describing a module from a different course
followUps: Have you looked into how students get involved in research projects here?

question: Tell us about a book, article, or documentary about biology that you've engaged with outside the classroom and what it made you think about.
lookingFor: Genuine intellectual engagement beyond the syllabus — the ability to say what an idea challenged or extended, not just summarise it.

bullets: Name a specific work and give a specific idea from it, not just the title
Explain what it challenged, confirmed, or made you wonder about
Show it connected to something you were already thinking about
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I read The Selfish Gene after my teacher recommended it, and the part that stopped me was the idea that an organism is essentially a survival machine built by its genes. I'd been thinking of evolution from the organism's perspective, and this flipped it completely. What I kept turning over was the extended phenotype concept — that genes express themselves through behaviour and even the environment the organism creates, like a beaver dam. It raised questions I hadn't thought to ask, like where the organism ends and the gene's influence begins. I'd argue that framing is still incomplete because it underweights developmental plasticity, but it forced me to think more rigorously.

commonMistakes: Naming a book and only summarising its content with no personal reaction
Choosing a very popular text and giving the obvious interpretation
Mentioning something clearly read very recently with no genuine engagement
followUps: Did it change how you think about any topic in your A-level Biology?

question: Can you explain natural selection in your own words — without using the phrase 'survival of the fittest'?
lookingFor: A clear mechanistic explanation that captures heritable variation, differential reproduction, and environmental context — not just a slogan.

bullets: Identify the three necessary conditions: heritable variation, differential survival/reproduction, environmental pressure
Explain that selection acts on traits, not on individuals deliberately adapting
Optionally note a specific example to anchor the explanation
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Natural selection works because individuals within a population aren't identical — there's variation in their traits, and some of that variation is heritable. When the environment favours certain traits over others, the individuals carrying those traits tend to survive longer and reproduce more, passing the traits on. Over generations the population shifts. What matters is that the organism doesn't choose to change — the change is statistical, happening across a population over time. A good example is antibiotic resistance: bacteria with slightly different cell-wall proteins are better able to survive a drug; they reproduce; their descendants inherit that resistance; and eventually the population is dominated by resistant strains.

commonMistakes: Saying organisms 'adapt themselves' or 'decide' to change — implying intent
Confusing natural selection with evolution as a whole
Leaving out heritability, so the mechanism doesn't actually explain change over generations
followUps: Can natural selection act on a trait that only affects an organism after it has reproduced?

question: Walk us through how information in a gene becomes a functional protein.
lookingFor: A clear, accurate account of transcription and translation with the key molecules named — shows command of core molecular biology.

bullets: Name the two stages: transcription (DNA to mRNA) and translation (mRNA to protein)
Identify the key players: RNA polymerase, mRNA, ribosomes, tRNA, codons, amino acids
Mention that post-translational folding or modification is often required for function
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The process has two main stages. In transcription, RNA polymerase binds to the gene's promoter region and unwinds the DNA double helix, using one strand as a template to build a complementary mRNA molecule. That mRNA then travels out of the nucleus to a ribosome. In translation, the ribosome reads the mRNA three bases at a time — each triplet codon codes for a specific amino acid, brought in by a matching tRNA molecule. The ribosome links the amino acids together in sequence to form a polypeptide chain. That chain then folds — often with the help of chaperone proteins — into the three-dimensional shape that gives the protein its function. Some proteins also need post-translational modifications like phosphorylation before they're active.

commonMistakes: Mixing up the roles of mRNA and tRNA
Stopping at the polypeptide chain without mentioning folding
Saying DNA codes directly for the protein without the mRNA intermediate
followUps: What would happen if a single base in the DNA sequence were changed?

question: What is the difference between mitosis and meiosis, and why does each type of division matter?
lookingFor: A clear comparison of outcomes and purpose — not just a list of stages, but an understanding of why each process exists.

bullets: State that mitosis produces two genetically identical diploid cells; meiosis produces four haploid cells with genetic variation
Link mitosis to growth, repair, and asexual reproduction; meiosis to gamete formation and sexual reproduction
Mention crossing over as the source of genetic variation in meiosis
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Mitosis produces two daughter cells that are genetically identical to the parent and to each other — it's what your body uses for growth and tissue repair, so precision and fidelity matter more than variation. Meiosis produces four haploid cells with half the chromosome number, and it deliberately introduces variation through crossing over, where homologous chromosomes exchange segments, and through independent assortment. That variation is the whole point — it means the gametes produced are genetically unique, and fertilisation combines two unique sets to produce a new individual. It's why sexual reproduction is such a powerful evolutionary mechanism, even though it's metabolically expensive compared to cloning yourself.

commonMistakes: Listing phases (prophase, metaphase...) without explaining the functional difference
Forgetting to mention the haploid outcome of meiosis
Not connecting each type to its biological purpose
followUps: Why might an error in meiosis have more serious consequences than one in mitosis?

question: How do enzymes work, and what happens when you raise the temperature well above an enzyme's optimum?
lookingFor: A mechanistic explanation of catalysis via active site shape, plus an accurate account of denaturation — not just 'the enzyme dies'.

bullets: Explain that enzymes lower activation energy by binding substrate at the active site, forming an enzyme-substrate complex
Connect the active site's shape to its specificity
Describe denaturation as an irreversible structural change caused by broken bonds — not death
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Enzymes are biological catalysts that speed up reactions by lowering the activation energy needed for them to proceed. The substrate binds to the active site, which has a complementary shape, forming an enzyme-substrate complex — this interaction strains chemical bonds in the substrate and makes them easier to break or form. The enzyme is released unchanged and can catalyse the next reaction. If you raise the temperature well above the optimum, the increased kinetic energy breaks the hydrogen bonds and other non-covalent interactions that hold the enzyme's tertiary structure together. The active site loses its shape, the substrate can no longer bind properly, and the change is irreversible — the enzyme is denatured, not just slowed down. That's a crucial distinction: you can't cool it back down and recover activity.

commonMistakes: Saying the enzyme 'dies' at high temperature — denaturation is a structural, not biological event
Describing the lock-and-key model as the only explanation without acknowledging induced fit
Confusing reversible inhibition with irreversible denaturation
followUps: How does enzyme behaviour change if you add a competitive inhibitor instead of raising the temperature?

question: If you had to explain to a curious 14-year-old why plants don't eat food the way animals do, what would you say — and what makes photosynthesis genuinely clever?
lookingFor: A conceptually accurate and enthusiastic explanation that goes beyond rote recitation — shows understanding of the underlying chemistry and an ability to communicate it.

bullets: Explain that plants capture light energy and use it to convert CO2 and water into glucose — making their own fuel
Distinguish this from heterotrophic nutrition without using jargon the 14-year-old wouldn't know
Identify something genuinely clever: the efficiency of the light-harvesting complexes, the elegance of the Calvin cycle, or the chlorophyll absorption spectrum
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'd say plants are basically solar panels with a chemistry lab attached. Instead of getting energy by breaking down other organisms' molecules, they use sunlight to power a reaction that stitches carbon dioxide from the air together with water from the soil, building glucose from scratch. Animals can't do that — we have to eat things that already contain stored energy. What I find genuinely clever is the light-harvesting complex: plants pack multiple pigment molecules together so that when one absorbs a photon, the energy hops along the chain until it reaches the reaction centre and drives chemistry. It's a biological energy relay system that's more efficient than anything we've engineered at a comparable scale.

commonMistakes: Reciting the equation without explaining what any part of it means
Not engaging with what makes it interesting or impressive
Overcomplicating the explanation with jargon while missing the core idea
followUps: What limits how fast a plant can photosynthesise?

question: What is homeostasis, and can you give a specific example of a negative feedback loop in a mammal?
lookingFor: An accurate definition of homeostasis as maintenance of a stable internal environment, plus a clear mechanistic account of a feedback loop — not just a list of steps.

bullets: Define homeostasis as the maintenance of a stable internal environment despite external change
Identify the components: receptor, control centre, effector
Walk through a specific example: blood glucose, body temperature, or blood CO2 regulation
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Homeostasis is how the body keeps its internal environment within narrow limits even when external conditions change — things like temperature, blood pH, and glucose concentration. Negative feedback is the key mechanism: when a variable drifts from the set point, the deviation triggers a response that pushes it back. Blood glucose regulation is a clean example. After a meal, blood glucose rises; beta cells in the pancreas detect this and secrete insulin; insulin signals cells to take up glucose, and the liver to convert it to glycogen for storage; blood glucose falls back toward the set point. If it drops too far, alpha cells release glucagon, which reverses the process. It's called negative feedback because the response opposes the change rather than amplifying it.

commonMistakes: Describing feedback without identifying what is being detected and what is doing the detecting
Calling positive feedback 'homeostatic' — positive feedback drives a system away from the set point
Listing steps without explaining why the system returns to baseline
followUps: Can you think of a situation in the body where positive feedback is actually useful?

question: Why is energy transfer between trophic levels so inefficient, and what are the consequences of that for food web structure?
lookingFor: A mechanistic explanation of the ~10% rule with the correct reasons behind it, plus an implication for ecosystem structure or human food choices.

bullets: State that roughly 10% of energy passes from one trophic level to the next
Explain where the rest goes: heat lost in respiration, materials not consumed, waste
Connect this to why food chains are short and why top predators are rare
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: At each trophic level, only about ten percent of the energy stored in biomass is transferred to the next level. The rest is lost mainly as heat during cellular respiration, since organisms aren't perfectly efficient machines. Some biomass is never eaten, some passes through as waste, and some is tied up in parts like bone that predators can't digest. Because of this cascade of losses, by the time you're at a top predator there's very little energy left to support it, which is why food chains rarely extend beyond four or five links and why apex predators are always sparse relative to their prey. It also explains why eating plants is far more energy-efficient than eating meat — you're harvesting energy one step closer to its source.

commonMistakes: Saying energy is 'used up' without specifying it's lost as heat
Not connecting the inefficiency to the structure of ecosystems or the rarity of top predators
Confusing energy flow with nutrient cycling, which is very different
followUps: What would happen to an ecosystem if a species in the middle of a food web went extinct?

question: Two parents who are both carriers of a recessive genetic condition have children. Can you predict the probabilities, and explain why any child might be unaffected even though both parents are carriers?
lookingFor: Correct Mendelian ratios with clear reasoning, not just a memorised answer — and an understanding of what 'carrier' actually means at the allele level.

bullets: Identify carrier parents as heterozygous (Aa × Aa)
State the 1:2:1 genotype ratio and the 3:1 phenotype ratio that results
Explain that unaffected children include both AA (homozygous dominant) and Aa (carrier) genotypes
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: If both parents are carriers, they're each heterozygous — one working copy of the gene and one faulty copy. The cross is Aa by Aa, and each child has an equal chance of inheriting any combination. The possible outcomes are AA, Aa, Aa, and aa — so roughly one in four children will have two faulty copies and be affected, two in four will be carriers like their parents, and one in four will have two working copies and neither be affected nor carry the condition. An unaffected child isn't affected because they've inherited at least one dominant, functional allele, which is enough to produce sufficient protein. What's interesting is that the carrier children look identical to the unaffected ones — you can't tell from appearance alone, which is why genetic testing matters in clinical settings.

commonMistakes: Giving the 3:1 ratio without explaining what the three phenotypically normal children actually are
Confusing dominant and recessive in the explanation
Not distinguishing genotype from phenotype
followUps: How would the ratios change if the condition were X-linked recessive?

question: Why is antibiotic resistance such a serious problem, and how does it arise from basic evolutionary principles?
lookingFor: A mechanistic evolutionary account — variation, selection, heritability — applied to bacteria, plus genuine engagement with the scale of the problem.

bullets: Connect resistance to natural selection: variation exists in bacterial populations, antibiotics select for resistant variants, resistance is heritable
Mention horizontal gene transfer as an additional mechanism that speeds spread between species
Acknowledge the clinical and public health stakes
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Antibiotic resistance is a textbook case of natural selection working in real time. Bacterial populations are enormous and mutate rapidly, so even before antibiotics existed, rare individuals had mutations affecting their cell walls or efflux pumps that made them slightly harder to kill. Expose the population to an antibiotic and you remove most of the bacteria but leave the resistant ones to reproduce. Their offspring inherit the resistance, and the population shifts. What makes this worse than ordinary evolution is horizontal gene transfer — bacteria can pass resistance genes directly to other bacteria, even across species, which means resistance spreads much faster than you'd predict from mutation alone. The stakes are severe: without effective antibiotics, routine surgery becomes life-threatening, and infections that are currently treatable could become fatal again.

commonMistakes: Saying bacteria 'become resistant' or 'learn to resist' — implying intentional adaptation
Ignoring horizontal gene transfer when explaining speed of spread
Treating this as a theoretical problem rather than an urgent public health crisis
followUps: What are some strategies we might use to slow the development of antibiotic resistance?

question: What is CRISPR-Cas9 and why has it generated so much interest — and controversy?
lookingFor: A clear, accurate account of the mechanism plus genuine engagement with why it matters and what ethical concerns it raises — not just a recitation of headlines.

bullets: Explain CRISPR-Cas9 as a precision gene-editing tool using a guide RNA to direct Cas9 to a specific DNA sequence
Articulate why it's transformative: cheap, fast, and more precise than previous methods
Engage with the controversy: therapeutic potential versus germline editing and equity concerns
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: CRISPR-Cas9 is a gene-editing system adapted from a bacterial immune mechanism. The Cas9 protein acts as a molecular scissors, but it needs a guide RNA to direct it to the right location in the genome — you design the guide to match the sequence you want to cut. The cell then repairs the cut in a way you can predict and exploit, allowing you to disable, correct, or insert genes with unprecedented precision. The excitement is justified: it's far cheaper and faster than earlier gene-editing tools, which has opened the door to potential treatments for conditions like sickle cell anaemia. The controversy centres on germline editing — changes made to an embryo will be inherited by all descendants. The He Jiankui case, where edited babies were born, showed the field moved faster than its ethical framework. Questions about who benefits, who decides what counts as a disease worth editing, and how we prevent it becoming a tool of enhancement rather than treatment are genuinely hard.

commonMistakes: Describing CRISPR vaguely as 'cutting and pasting DNA' without explaining the mechanism
Only mentioning the excitement without engaging with the controversy
Getting the He Jiankui case wrong or treating ethics as an afterthought
followUps: Do you think there are uses of CRISPR that should be permanently prohibited? Why?

question: Why does biodiversity matter beyond the fact that we find species interesting or beautiful?
lookingFor: Substantive ecological and functional arguments — ecosystem services, resilience, genetic diversity — rather than purely emotional or aesthetic ones.

bullets: Argue from ecosystem function: species play roles in nutrient cycling, pollination, pest control, clean water
Introduce the concept of resilience: diverse ecosystems absorb disturbance better than simplified ones
Mention genetic diversity as a resource for medicine, food security, and adaptation to future environments
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Biodiversity matters functionally, not just aesthetically. Every ecosystem provides services that humans depend on — pollination of crops, decomposition of organic matter, water filtration, carbon storage — and those services are underpinned by the interactions between species. Remove enough species and you start losing the functions, sometimes in ways that are hard to predict because food webs have knock-on effects. Diverse ecosystems are also more resilient: if one species of pollinator disappears, another may fill the niche, but in a monoculture-equivalent system there's no backup. There's also a resource argument — wild species have been the source of most of our antibiotics and many medicines, and genetic diversity within crop species is our best insurance against a pathogen that wipes out a monoculture. We don't know what we'll need, so we can't afford to be careless about what we lose.

commonMistakes: Arguing only from a moral or emotional standpoint without functional reasoning
Treating biodiversity and ecosystem services as separate rather than connected
Ignoring the genetic diversity argument for agriculture and medicine
followUps: If you had to prioritise conservation funding, how would you decide which species or habitats to protect first?

question: What is osmosis, and why does it matter for living cells?
lookingFor: A precise mechanistic definition of osmosis — net movement of water down its water potential gradient across a partially permeable membrane — and a concrete biological consequence.

bullets: Define osmosis as the net movement of water molecules across a partially permeable membrane from a region of higher to lower water potential
Explain that water potential is lowered by dissolved solutes
Give a clear biological example of consequence: plant cell turgor, crenation of red blood cells, or kidney function
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Osmosis is the net movement of water molecules across a partially permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to one of lower water potential. Dissolved solutes lower water potential, so a concentrated solution draws water toward it. For living cells this matters enormously. In plants, water moving into vacuoles by osmosis pushes the cell contents against the wall, creating turgor pressure that keeps stems upright — without it, the plant wilts. In animal cells there's no wall, so the consequences are more dramatic: put a red blood cell in distilled water and water enters until it bursts; in a very concentrated solution it loses water and crenates, shrinking and losing function. That's why intravenous fluids in medicine are carefully isotonic — matched to blood plasma so cells neither swell nor shrink.

commonMistakes: Saying water moves from low to high concentration — this conflates concentration with water potential
Not specifying that the membrane must be partially permeable, not fully permeable
Giving a definition but no biological consequence
followUps: How do cells in saltwater fish deal with the osmotic challenge of their environment?

question: How does a nerve impulse travel along a neurone, and how does the signal cross a synapse?
lookingFor: A coherent account of the action potential mechanism and synaptic transmission — accurate on the key ions and molecules without needing to list every protein.

bullets: Explain resting potential and describe depolarisation: Na+ ions rush in, membrane potential reverses
Describe repolarisation via K+ outflow and the refractory period that enforces one-way travel
Explain synaptic transmission: vesicles release neurotransmitter, binds postsynaptic receptors, may trigger a new action potential
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: At rest, a neurone maintains a negative charge inside relative to outside — the resting potential — mainly kept by sodium-potassium pumps. When a stimulus reaches threshold, sodium channels open and Na+ floods in, making the inside briefly positive — depolarisation. This triggers the next section of membrane to depolarise, propagating the impulse along the axon. Potassium channels then open, K+ flows out, restoring the negative charge — repolarisation. The brief refractory period, when the membrane can't fire again, ensures the impulse travels in one direction. At the synapse, the action potential reaches a terminal button, calcium ions enter, and vesicles fuse with the membrane to release neurotransmitter into the synaptic cleft. The neurotransmitter binds receptors on the postsynaptic membrane, potentially triggering a new action potential. The neurotransmitter is then broken down or reabsorbed to end the signal.

commonMistakes: Confusing which ion carries which part of the action potential — Na+ in for depolarisation, K+ out for repolarisation
Describing the synapse without mentioning vesicles, calcium, or receptor binding
Not explaining how the refractory period ensures one-directional travel
followUps: How do drugs that block acetylcholinesterase affect synaptic transmission?

question: Are viruses alive? And how does the human immune system tell the difference between a virus-infected cell and a healthy one?
lookingFor: Genuine engagement with the definitional question about life, plus a clear mechanistic account of how cytotoxic T cells or MHC-I presentation works.

bullets: Engage seriously with the 'are viruses alive' question — they meet some criteria but not all, and this is a genuine scientific debate
Explain MHC class I presentation: infected cells display viral peptide fragments on their surface
Describe how cytotoxic T cells recognise these and destroy the cell before more virions are produced
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The alive question is genuinely interesting rather than settled. Viruses have genetic material and evolve, which ticks some boxes, but they can't replicate independently, have no metabolism, and don't maintain homeostasis. Most biologists treat them as biological entities at the edge of life rather than living organisms — the boundary depends on how you define life, which is itself contested. As for recognition, every nucleated cell in the body displays fragments of the proteins it's currently making on MHC class I molecules — a kind of molecular shop window. If a cell is infected by a virus, viral peptides appear in that window. Cytotoxic T cells scan these displays constantly; when they detect a non-self peptide, they bind to the cell and trigger apoptosis — killing the cell before it can release more virions. It's a system that sacrifices individual cells to protect the organism, which is a fascinating trade-off.

commonMistakes: Giving a definitive 'no, viruses aren't alive' without engaging with the nuance
Describing antibodies attacking viruses without addressing how infected cells are recognised — these are different arms of the immune system
Confusing MHC class I (all nucleated cells) with MHC class II (antigen-presenting cells)
followUps: How does HIV evade this immune surveillance?

question: There are countries that allow GM crops and countries that do not. What considerations would you weigh if you were advising a government on this?
lookingFor: Structured, balanced ethical reasoning that draws on biology, economics, and public health — not a position paper but an evidence-based analysis of trade-offs.

bullets: Identify the genuine benefits: yield, pest resistance, drought tolerance, nutritional enhancement
Acknowledge the legitimate concerns: biodiversity, corporate control of seed supply, unknown long-term ecological effects
Show you can hold the tension rather than dismissing either side
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'd try to separate empirical questions from value questions, because they often get mixed up in this debate. On the empirical side, there's strong evidence that many approved GM crops are safe to eat and can increase yield and reduce pesticide use. Golden Rice is a case where the nutritional benefit in vitamin A-deficient populations is real and significant. But legitimate concerns exist: herbicide-resistant crops have driven the adoption of herbicide-tolerant weeds; cross-pollination with wild relatives can have unpredictable effects; and concentration of seed patents in a few companies raises food sovereignty concerns that aren't about the science at all. I'd advise case-by-case assessment rather than a blanket position, robust regulatory testing before approval, and transparent labelling so consumers can make informed choices. I'd also distinguish strongly between using GM crops in agriculture and releasing gene-edited organisms into wild ecosystems, which carries a different risk profile entirely.

commonMistakes: Treating the debate as purely scientific when the core disagreements are political and ethical
Dismissing anti-GM concerns as simply irrational
Failing to distinguish different types of genetic modification, which have very different risk profiles
followUps: Does your answer change if the country is facing a food security crisis?

question: If you wanted to test whether a particular fertiliser improves plant growth, how would you design an experiment that gives you reliable results?
lookingFor: Clear thinking about controls, variables, replication, and what counts as a valid measure of growth — not a perfect protocol but a systematic approach.

bullets: Identify the independent variable (fertiliser presence/dose), the dependent variable (a measurable aspect of growth), and the variables to control
Explain the need for a control group and for replication across enough plants to get statistical power
Mention how you'd standardise conditions to avoid confounding variables
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'd start by choosing a measurable dependent variable — height, dry mass, or leaf area are all defensible, each with trade-offs. The independent variable is whether the fertiliser is applied, or at what concentration if I'm testing dose-response. I'd set up two groups of plants: an experimental group receiving the fertiliser and a control group receiving everything except the fertiliser — same soil type, pot size, watering regime, light exposure, and species of plant. I'd use at least ten plants per group to allow for natural variation and give the statistics some power. I'd measure at standardised intervals and record results blinded if possible. At the end I'd use an appropriate statistical test to determine whether any difference in growth is greater than you'd expect by chance. The key question throughout is: is there any other variable that differs between the groups that could explain the result?

commonMistakes: Describing a method without identifying what is being controlled
Using only one plant per condition — no replication means no statistical validity
Not specifying a measurable, operational definition of 'growth'
followUps: How would you handle the fact that plant growth is inherently variable even within the same species?

question: What is epigenetics, and can you give an example of why it matters?
lookingFor: A clear explanation of what epigenetics is — heritable changes in gene expression without changes to the DNA sequence — with an accurate example and some awareness of why it was surprising.

bullets: Define epigenetics as heritable changes in gene expression that don't alter the underlying DNA sequence
Name a mechanism: DNA methylation or histone modification
Give an example with genuine implications — twin studies, imprinting, or environmental programming
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Epigenetics refers to changes in how genes are expressed — switched on or off — that can be heritable without any change to the actual DNA sequence. The main mechanisms are DNA methylation, where methyl groups attached to cytosines tend to silence nearby genes, and histone modification, which changes how tightly DNA is wrapped and therefore how accessible it is for transcription. What makes this significant is that it challenged the simple one-way model of genetics: the idea that the genome is a fixed instruction set. An example I find compelling is genomic imprinting — whether you inherit a functioning copy of a gene can depend on whether it came from your mother or your father, because the two copies are differentially methylated in the germline. This means identical sequences have different effects depending on their origin. Environmental factors can also alter methylation patterns, which raises fascinating questions about how experience might shape gene expression across generations — though that research is still contested.

commonMistakes: Confusing epigenetics with Lamarckian inheritance — they're related concepts but not the same
Describing epigenetics as permanent when many modifications are reversible
Overstating the evidence for transgenerational epigenetic inheritance in humans, which is still an active research question
followUps: How might epigenetic changes complicate the interpretation of twin studies?

question: Tell us about a practical or lab experience you've found genuinely interesting and what it taught you beyond the technique itself.
lookingFor: A specific example with a specific observation — ideally one where something didn't go as expected — showing the ability to reflect rather than just describe.

bullets: Name a specific practical, not a generic 'we did experiments in class'
Describe what actually happened and what you observed
Reflect on what it taught you about the process of science, not just the content
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: We did a gel electrophoresis practical to separate DNA fragments by size, and mine came out smeared rather than as clean bands. I initially assumed I'd made an error, but when I compared notes with others who had similar results, we traced it to overloading the wells. That was unexpectedly useful: it showed me that results you'd describe as 'failed' often carry real information about what went wrong and why, and they require the same reasoning as successful ones. It also made me think about experimental design differently — an anomalous result isn't an obstacle, it's a question you haven't answered yet. I read a bit more about the method afterward and learned about techniques like Southern blotting that build on the same principle, which I hadn't come across in class.

commonMistakes: Describing a practical that went perfectly with nothing to reflect on
Staying entirely at the level of 'it was interesting' without saying why
Choosing an example that shows no independent thinking beyond following instructions
followUps: What would you do differently if you ran that experiment again?

question: At a cellular level, what goes wrong in cancer, and why is it so difficult to treat?
lookingFor: A clear mechanistic account of uncontrolled cell division — mutations in oncogenes and tumour suppressor genes — plus genuine engagement with why targeting cancer without harming healthy tissue is hard.

bullets: Explain that cancer arises from mutations that deregulate the cell cycle — oncogenes accelerate division, tumour suppressor mutations remove braking mechanisms
Note that it takes multiple mutations to produce a malignant cell — the hallmarks of cancer
Engage honestly with why treatment is hard: tumour heterogeneity, the similarity between cancer cells and normal cells, and resistance emergence
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Cancer is fundamentally a disease of uncontrolled cell division caused by accumulated mutations. Normally the cell cycle is tightly regulated: proto-oncogenes promote division and tumour suppressor genes act as brakes. Mutations that activate proto-oncogenes into oncogenes push the accelerator down; mutations that disable tumour suppressors like p53 remove the brakes. It typically takes multiple mutations across several of these control points before a cell becomes truly malignant. Treatment is difficult for a few interconnected reasons. Cancer cells are derived from your own cells, so they share most of the same molecular machinery — any drug toxic to cancer cells tends to be toxic to fast-dividing healthy cells too, which is why chemotherapy causes the side effects it does. Beyond that, solid tumours are not homogeneous: different cells within the same tumour carry different mutations, so a treatment that kills ninety percent of the cells selects for the resistant ten percent, which then repopulate the tumour. Newer targeted therapies are more precise but still face this resistance problem.

commonMistakes: Describing cancer as 'cells dividing too fast' without explaining the molecular basis
Treating all cancers as the same rather than acknowledging the diversity of the disease
Not engaging with why treatment is genuinely difficult beyond 'it's complex'
followUps: How do immunotherapy approaches differ from chemotherapy in how they target cancer?

subtopic: Biological question you'd want to answer
question: If you could design any experiment you wanted — no constraints on funding or technology — what biological question would you try to answer, and why?
lookingFor: Genuine scientific curiosity expressed as a researchable question — creativity, enthusiasm, and some understanding of why the question matters.

bullets: Pose a real biological question, not just 'cure cancer' or 'end disease' which are goals not questions
Explain why the question is interesting and what we don't yet know
Sketch at least roughly how you'd approach it
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'd want to understand how the gut microbiome influences brain development in early life. We know from animal studies that germ-free mice show altered anxiety behaviours, and there are associations in human data between microbiome composition and neurodevelopmental conditions, but the causal mechanisms are unclear. I'd want to run a longitudinal study tracking microbiome composition in infants from birth through to age five alongside detailed neurodevelopmental assessments, while also doing mechanistic work in model organisms to identify which specific microbial metabolites cross the gut-brain axis and what they do to neural circuitry. The question matters because if the relationship is causal and if there are specific windows where it matters most, it opens up the possibility of early interventions that are cheap and low-risk — just shifting microbiome composition — rather than pharmaceutical ones.

commonMistakes: Proposing a technology rather than a question — 'I'd sequence every genome' is not a research question
Proposing something with an obvious answer already in the literature, suggesting limited reading
Being so vague that it's not clear what would actually be tested
followUps: What result would most surprise you, and what would it mean if you got it?

question: The mitochondria in your cells contain their own DNA and resemble bacteria in several ways. What does that tell us, and what broader idea does it illustrate?
lookingFor: Knowledge and understanding of endosymbiotic theory — including the evidence for it — and the ability to place it in a broader evolutionary context.

bullets: State the endosymbiotic theory: mitochondria and chloroplasts were once free-living prokaryotes engulfed by ancestral eukaryotic cells
Name the key lines of evidence: own circular DNA, double membrane, size comparable to bacteria, divide by binary fission, own ribosomes
Connect it to the broader theme of evolutionary innovation through integration rather than just competition
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Mitochondria show multiple lines of evidence that they were once independent bacteria — they have their own circular DNA, their own ribosomes that are more similar to bacterial ribosomes than eukaryotic ones, a double membrane consistent with having been engulfed, and they divide by binary fission on a separate cycle from the cell itself. Lynn Margulis proposed the endosymbiotic theory to explain all of this, and it's now very well supported. The broader idea it illustrates is that major evolutionary transitions can happen through integration — what started as a host-parasite or host-prey relationship became a mutualism so complete that the former bacterium is now an organelle that the host cell can't live without. It's a reminder that evolution doesn't only work through competition and selection of individuals; sometimes the most significant leaps happen when organisms merge and cooperate at a level that creates something genuinely new.

commonMistakes: Describing endosymbiosis as a theory in the weak sense — it's very strongly supported evidence
Only stating the theory without any of the evidence for it
Missing the evolutionary significance and treating it as a biology-trivia answer
followUps: Why do you think most mitochondrial genes have moved to the nucleus over evolutionary time?

question: We're told it's a golden age for biology. Do you agree, and what makes you excited about where the field is heading?
lookingFor: Informed enthusiasm — specific developments the applicant has engaged with, a sense of what feels genuinely new, and the ability to connect it to their own interests.

bullets: Name specific recent advances — not just 'technology is better' but concrete developments like single-cell sequencing, synthetic biology, or mRNA vaccines
Explain what is genuinely new about the current moment compared to previous eras
Connect at least one development to something you personally find exciting or want to work on
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I do think it's a genuine inflection point, and the reason is convergence: tools from computing, chemistry, and biology are developing together in ways that weren't possible before. Single-cell RNA sequencing has completely changed our ability to map cell types and states — you can now see the heterogeneity within what we thought was a uniform tissue. AlphaFold's prediction of protein structures from sequence alone has removed a bottleneck that was slowing structural biology for decades. And mRNA vaccines showed, in real time and at global scale, that you can move from sequence to deployed therapeutic in under a year. What excites me personally is synthetic biology — the idea of designing biological systems with intention, rather than just characterising natural ones. We're starting to engineer genetic circuits and even minimal cells from scratch. That raises hard questions about what life is, which I find as interesting as the applications.

commonMistakes: Vague enthusiasm about 'curing diseases' with no specific developments named
Only mentioning things covered in the A-level syllabus rather than reading beyond it
Not connecting any of it to a personal interest — the question is partly about you, not just the field
followUps: Which of those developments do you think has the most significant unanswered questions still attached to it?
